scanTypes.name: TCP SYN scan
scanTypes.description: Half-open scan — sends SYN, observes SYN/ACK or RST without completing the handshake. Default when run as root; fast and relatively quiet.

scanTypes.name: TCP connect scan
scanTypes.description: Full TCP three-way handshake using the OS connect() call. Used when raw sockets aren't available (unprivileged user).

scanTypes.name: UDP scan
scanTypes.description: Sends UDP probes; open ports are inferred from no response, closed from ICMP port-unreachable. Slow but essential for DNS, SNMP, NTP.

scanTypes.name: ACK scan
scanTypes.description: Maps firewall rule sets — distinguishes filtered (no response) from unfiltered (RST) ports. Doesn't determine open vs closed.

scanTypes.name: Ping sweep
scanTypes.description: Host discovery only — no port scan. The right tool for "what's alive on this subnet?".

scanTypes.name: Treat all hosts as up
scanTypes.description: Skip host discovery. Use when ICMP is blocked but you know the host is there.

scanTypes.name: Service / version detection
scanTypes.description: Probes open ports and matches banner replies against the nmap-service-probes database to identify the running software and version.

scanTypes.name: OS fingerprinting
scanTypes.description: TCP/IP stack fingerprinting against the nmap-os-db. Needs at least one open and one closed port for accuracy.

scanTypes.name: Default script scan
scanTypes.description: Runs the "safe" and "default" NSE script categories. Equivalent to --script=default.

scanTypes.name: Aggressive scan
scanTypes.description: Bundle: -sV -sC -O --traceroute. The "give me everything" flag — loud, but extremely informative.

timing.name: Paranoid
timing.description: 5-minute waits between probes — for IDS evasion. Days to scan a single host.

timing.name: Sneaky
timing.description: 15-second probe interval. Still extremely slow, used to fly under detection thresholds.

timing.name: Polite
timing.description: Reduces bandwidth and target load — useful for fragile or production hosts.

timing.name: Normal (default)
timing.description: Balanced speed and reliability. Sensible default for most networks.

timing.name: Aggressive
timing.description: Faster timeouts, higher concurrency. Standard for assessments on a fast LAN — recommended for most pentests.

timing.name: Insane
timing.description: Maximum speed; sacrifices accuracy. Misses ports on slower or congested links.

outputFormats.description: Normal human-readable output (what you see on screen).
Greppable output — one line per host. Easy to parse with awk/grep.
XML output. Imports into Metasploit, BloodHound, dradis, every reporting tool.
All three formats at once (basename.nmap, .gnmap, .xml). Default choice during engagements.

nseScripts.name: vuln
nseScripts.description: Run all vulnerability-checking scripts (--script vuln). Includes Heartbleed, MS17-010, ShellShock, ms08-067, etc.

nseScripts.name: auth
nseScripts.description: Authentication-related — default credentials, Kerberos enumeration, anonymous logins.

nseScripts.name: discovery
nseScripts.description: Service and topology discovery — DNS zone transfer attempts, SNMP enumeration, SMB share enumeration.

nseScripts.name: brute
nseScripts.description: Brute-force scripts against many protocols (SSH, FTP, HTTP-form, RDP, SMB). Loud — get authorisation first.

nseScripts.name: smb-enum-shares
nseScripts.description: List SMB shares on a target — workhorse for AD reconnaissance.

nseScripts.name: ssl-enum-ciphers
nseScripts.description: Enumerate accepted TLS ciphers and protocol versions. Surfaces weak/legacy crypto.

nseScripts.name: http-title
nseScripts.description: Pull the <title> from every web service — fast way to triage a list of hosts.

nseScripts.name: http-enum
nseScripts.description: Probe a server for known directories, admin panels, and CMS install paths.

nseScripts.name: dns-brute
nseScripts.description: Subdomain brute-forcing using a wordlist — basic OSINT for an external-facing target.

recipes.title: Discover live hosts on a /24
recipes.details: Combines ICMP echo, timestamp, TCP SYN to common ports, and TCP ACK. Catches more hosts than a default ping sweep, especially when ICMP is blocked.

recipes.title: Top-1000 TCP scan with service detection
recipes.details: The bread-and-butter assessment scan: SYN scan against the most common 1000 ports, version detection, default scripts, output in all formats.

recipes.title: Full TCP port scan
recipes.details: All 65535 TCP ports — required for finding services on non-standard ports. --min-rate caps per-second packet rate so the scan finishes in reasonable time.

recipes.title: UDP top ports
recipes.details: UDP scans are slow — limiting to the top 50 ports keeps it tractable and still catches DNS, SNMP, NTP, NetBIOS, IKE, RDP-UDP.

recipes.title: Vulnerability sweep
recipes.details: Runs every NSE script in the vuln category. Expect noise and false positives; treat output as leads, not as proven vulnerabilities.

recipes.title: Web stack fingerprint across many hosts
recipes.details: Reads targets from a file. Pulls page titles, HTTP server headers, and accepted TLS ciphers — quick triage of a large IP list.

recipes.title: Stealthy slow scan
recipes.details: Polite timing, fragmented IP packets, and padded probe length to look less like default nmap. Useful when an IDS is suspected — though modern detection still flags it.

tips: Run as root (or with cap_net_raw) whenever possible — unprivileged nmap falls back to -sT (TCP connect), which is slower and noisier.
Always pair -p- with --min-rate or -T4 unless you're truly trying to be quiet — the default rate is very polite.
--reason annotates each port state with the evidence ("open: syn-ack", "filtered: no-response") which is invaluable when explaining results to a customer.
Use -oA basename during real engagements — the .gnmap and .xml outputs feed every downstream reporting tool.
Update scripts regularly: nmap --script-updatedb pulls new NSE script signatures from the local feed.
Test against scanme.nmap.org — Nmap project's authorised target for trying syntax safely.
limitations: Active scanning is detectable and noisy — IDSs, EDRs, and SIEMs will alert. Always have written authorisation before scanning anything you don't own.
Scan accuracy degrades on rate-limited or aggressively filtered networks; results need corroboration from passive sources.
Behind a load balancer or CDN, host fingerprints describe the edge, not the origin. The same IP may multiplex many backends.
Modern OSes randomise sequence numbers and time their RSTs to defeat naive OS detection — -O accuracy is much lower than it once was.
Containerised hosts and zero-trust deployments often expose nothing on the network — discovery must shift to the control plane (cloud APIs, k8s).
